stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver International Airport and Littleton church
stories.summary:
A traveler with a confirmed measles case passed through Denver International Airport and attended a Sunday service at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Littleton while infectious, Colorado public health officials announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived Feb. 21 on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami, landing at gate A83 at 9:15 a.m., and anyone in that area through noon may have been exposed. The traveler then attended services and a reception at St. Mary's, 6853 S. Prince St., between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday before departing DIA late that night on a return flight to Miami.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment said the passenger is fully vaccinated, which reduces but does not eliminate the risk of transmission. Passengers on both Frontier flights are to be notified directly by their state or local public health agency.

Arapahoe County Public Health Department and St. Mary's Catholic Church offered free measles vaccination clinics Wednesday for those potentially exposed at the church. Officials noted that receiving a vaccine within 72 hours of exposure may prevent infection or reduce symptom severity.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the most in a single year since at least 1996, with most cases occurring among unvaccinated individuals. One case had already been reported in Arapahoe County in 2026 prior to this exposure event.

stories.headline: Grass fire near Thornton injures five, forces evacuations and closes stretch of I-25
stories.summary:
A rapidly spreading grass fire ignited Feb. 25 at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton amid Red Flag conditions, prompting large-scale evacuations, road closures including a stretch of Interstate 25, and the precautionary evacuation of a nearby high school before crews contained the blaze at approximately 10 acres by 2:07 p.m. that afternoon. Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley described the fire as a "dynamic, rapidly spreading event" that challenged multiple departments across the north metro area.

Five people were injured in connection with the blaze — four firefighters and one civilian — though all injuries were described as non-critical. Resources from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams, and other agencies responded to the scene.

No residential structures were lost despite the fire burning along a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone. Some businesses along the southern perimeter sustained damage, though no structural losses were confirmed as of last report. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for re-ignition.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation. The incident comes as Colorado's Front Range continues to experience elevated fire danger due to dry conditions and persistent winds.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on suspicion of murder in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old man was arrested Friday in Kansas on a Denver homicide warrant in connection with a Valentine's Day shooting that killed one man and injured a woman in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue, Denver police said. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera was booked into the Wyandotte County Detention Center in Kansas City, Kansas, and faces charges including first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault, and a violent crime sentence enhancer, according to Denver court records.

Officers responded to the shooting at approximately 5:15 a.m. on Feb. 14 and found a woman shot in the ankle and a man who died at the scene. Investigators believe the shooting followed a fight that broke out at a nearby party. Police said at least two suspects fired shots, with Yriarte-Valera believed to have fired the fatal round.

A second suspect, believed to have shot and injured the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday. Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not yet been scheduled.

Anyone with information on the shooting is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Valentine's Day shooting outside Denver Aquarium
stories.summary:
Jason Trujillo, 19, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, have each pleaded guilty to second-degree murder in the fatal drive-by shooting of 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Denver Aquarium on Feb. 14, 2024, the Denver District Attorney's Office confirmed. Both are scheduled to be sentenced March 6.

Salazar, who had traveled to Denver from Pueblo with two friends for Valentine's Day, was shot by a masked individual firing from a black sedan as the group stood outside the aquarium. He was transported to a hospital, where he later died. Investigators connected the shooting to prior Facebook threats against Salazar and linked Vasquez to the scene through cell phone records and hotel surveillance footage.

Triujillo and Vasquez were arrested in May 2024 on first-degree murder charges after an anonymous tip helped identify Trujillo's involvement. The charges were ultimately reduced as part of the plea agreements. Vasquez entered his plea in December 2025 and Trujillo did so in January 2026.

The case drew significant attention in 2024 and a reward was offered as investigators worked to identify the suspects in the months following the killing.

stories.headline: Denver and Colorado business leaders respond to Supreme Court ruling striking down Trump tariffs
stories.summary:
Denver and Colorado business leaders issued statements welcoming clarity following a 6-3 U.S. Supreme Court decision striking down tariffs imposed by the Trump administration under an emergency powers law, including sweeping reciprocal tariffs levied on most trading partners. Leaders emphasized that predictability and stability are essential for the state's economy, which had seen impacts across sectors from wheat and automotive dealerships to bicycle retailers.

Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce President J.J. Ament said the ruling provides "important clarity" and establishes defined lanes for policymakers to pursue consistent trade policy. Colorado Chamber of Commerce President Loren Furman said many members had experienced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead costs, and uncertainty as a result of the tariffs.

Some business leaders cautioned that the ruling may not represent a permanent resolution. Colorado Competitive Council President Matthew Groves said the administration retains multiple avenues to impose tariffs and characterized the ruling as "temporary relief." Hours after the decision, President Trump announced a new executive order imposing an additional 10% global tariff rate and said he would pursue alternative methods.

The ruling centers on whether the administration had authority to impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Trump called the court's decision "incorrect" and signaled ongoing efforts to advance his trade agenda through other legal mechanisms.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan confirmed as first female director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to confirm Laura Clellan as permanent director of the agency, making her the first woman to lead the organization since CPW was formed through the merger of Colorado Wildlife and Colorado State Parks in 2011. Clellan had served as acting director since Dec. 1.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military service, including multiple overseas deployments, and previously served as adjutant general and executive director of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs. She achieved the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard and previously worked with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood. She was selected from approximately 150 applicants.

Clellan assumes the role during a contentious period for the agency. The federal government has halted Colorado's wolf reintroduction program indefinitely, stakeholder groups are divided over furbearer management changes, and some hunting advocacy groups have expressed concern that the Polis administration is excluding them from appointments and policy decisions.

Despite those tensions, Dan Gates of Coloradans for Responsible Wildlife Management said his group is confident Clellan will provide equitable transparency and accountability. The Rocky Mountain Wolf Project also expressed high expectations for her leadership.

stories.headline: Historic Loretto Heights Administration Building to become 50 rent-to-own affordable apartments in $70M project
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at the former Loretto Heights campus in southwest Denver into 50 rent-to-own apartments as part of a broader $70 million campus redevelopment effort, according to the Denver Business Journal. The project is rare in its rent-to-own structure, which is designed to provide a pathway to homeownership for residents in affordable housing units.

The first two floors of the building are planned for community programming, including a business incubator. A historic chapel connected to the building will be preserved and continue to operate as a wedding and event venue.

The Loretto Heights campus, a former Catholic college that closed in 1988, has been the subject of redevelopment planning for years. The Administration Building is among the most prominent structures on the site and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

The project reflects growing interest in adaptive reuse of historic Denver properties as the city seeks affordable housing solutions amid elevated construction costs. Denver's construction costs rank second-highest nationally, according to a separate 2026 Beck Group report cited by the Denver Business Journal.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority taps Urban Land Institute to chart future of Denver Pavilions
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has contracted with the Urban Land Institute for a $100,000 advisory panel visit April 13-17 to help determine the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots on the 16th Street Mall. The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall in December for $37 million and two nearby parking lots for $23 million after the previous owner, Gart Properties, defaulted on the property's loan.

The ULI panel will include five or six outside experts who will review materials submitted in response to two requests for proposals the DDDA has already issued — one seeking retail and entertainment leasing consultants and one seeking architecture and urban planning firms to reimagine the property. Submissions are due March 13, and a final report from the panel is expected approximately one month after the April visit.

The mall is currently approximately 60% leased and generates $5.3 million annually in rent, while parking operations bring in an additional $3.2 million per year. Gart Properties continues to manage the facility for $379,000, though the mall currently has no dedicated leasing team.

Denver Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the city hopes the process will generate both local and national attention for the site. He said the DDDA's goal is to transfer the property to private ownership as quickly as possible while preserving flexibility for redevelopment.

stories.headline: Globeville affordable housing development breaks ground on $132 million mixed-use project in Denver
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use development on a city-owned parcel in Denver's Globeville neighborhood, the site of a former car dealership. The project will deliver housing for families earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income.

The development represents a significant investment in one of Denver's most historically underserved neighborhoods, which has faced environmental and infrastructure challenges stemming from its industrial past and proximity to the I-25 and I-70 interchange. Affordable housing advocates have long called for targeted investment in Globeville as the surrounding areas have gentrified.

Details on the total number of units and a projected completion timeline were not provided in available reporting. The project is among the larger affordable housing investments to break ground in the Denver metro in recent months.

The development is part of a broader city effort to deploy publicly owned land parcels for affordable housing construction as Denver continues to grapple with housing affordability challenges.

stories.headline: Broncos reunite with John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator, reuniting him with head coach Sean Payton heading into the 2026 season, a source confirmed to The Denver Post. Morton previously held the same role with Denver in 2023 and 2024 before leaving for an offensive coordinator position with the Detroit Lions, who stripped him of play-calling duties midseason and fired him after a 9-8 finish.

Morton had already been working informally with the Broncos during their playoff run after Payton brought him back as a consultant. He helped install a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that produced a touchdown in Denver's 33-30 overtime divisional win over the Buffalo Bills.

The hire completes an offseason reshaping of Denver's offensive staff. Payton fired longtime offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days after the Broncos' AFC Championship Game loss, then promoted quarterbacks coach Davis Webb to offensive coordinator and elevated offensive-quality-control coach Logan Kilgore to the quarterbacks coach role. He also hired wide receivers coach Ronald Curry.

Morton's relationship with Payton dates to 2006, when he served as receivers coach in Payton's first season with the New Orleans Saints. The question of whether Morton or Webb will handle play-calling duties during games remains unresolved heading into the offseason.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, reaching market tipping point
stories.summary:
The number of heat pumps installed across five major Colorado utility service areas more than doubled in 2025 to 14,225 units, marking a twelvefold increase over three years, according to utility executives and industry data. Xcel Energy alone issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million in 2025, with Denver, Boulder and Littleton among the most active markets.

Industry representatives say 2025 represents an inflection point in which the technology has moved from niche to mainstream, driven by contractors becoming more comfortable with installations and improved ability to stack rebates and incentives to reduce upfront costs for consumers. Heat pumps can range from $15,000 to $30,000 depending on system size and home specifications.

Nationally, 3.642 million heat pumps were sold in the U.S. in 2025 compared with 3.28 million natural gas and oil furnaces, marking the first year heat pumps outsold gas heating equipment. The Platte River Power Authority, which serves Fort Collins, Longmont, Loveland and Estes Park, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple to 1,256.

The Energy Efficiency Business Coalition, a trade group, has set a goal of heat pumps capturing 50% to 80% of the total Colorado HVAC market by 2030. The rapid growth comes amid continued uncertainty about federal energy policy and the future of clean energy incentives.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship program to support and retain Black male educators in Denver metro schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, nicknamed "The Den," to provide professional development, networking and community support for Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The program is a collaboration between MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER program, the university's Office of Education Solutions, both school districts, and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

Program director Dr. Rashad Anderson said isolation is a primary driver of attrition among Black male educators, noting that Black men make up about 6% of Colorado's population and many are the only Black teacher in their school building. Anderson said the fellowship is designed to create a sense of brotherhood and shared professional identity among participants.

According to the National Association of State Boards of Education, 40% of Colorado school districts have no teacher of color, and 62% have no Black male teacher employed. Research consistently shows that students of all backgrounds benefit from exposure to teachers from diverse backgrounds, and that students of color are more likely to succeed academically when taught by teachers who share their identity.

The fellowship also aims to serve as a recruitment pipeline. Call Me MiSTER program participant Kaheem Heath, who has never had a Black male teacher in his own schooling, said programs like this are critical to inspiring the next generation of diverse educators.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
